All right, guys, so that is the first phase of this process is coming down to you. This is personal development. This is you guys figuring out those things. And so the biggest things, again, are being super clear on who you are serving. One of the biggest mistakes people make is they don't know who they're serving. So because of that, they're not serving anyone. So like Steven said, the more specific, the more laser targeted you are here, you can serve the better. And like I said, people that aren't necessarily that exact person will come to you as well. But we only want to focus on the people that we know we're going to be able to help the most. Sean Stevenson. How many of you know Sean Stevenson? He spoke at the second front of acting live. He's a little dude, three foot tall, in a wheelchair. And he told a really interesting story that he told a million times better than me. He talked to me. He's like, a lot of people ask, how do I know? My stuff helps everyone. I want to help everyone. I want to serve everyone. Who do I know? How do I know who to help and how to help them? It's a hard thing sometimes, right? Tell me, guys, know your stuff you've got could help everybody if you could just get it into their hands. And so he told the story of Funalaki Live. It was so cool. He said he talked about the people that go and they rescue people from when a plane crashes in the ocean, or a boat falls in the ocean, or crashes in the ocean. And the helicopters come to try to save the people. So the helicopter comes out. It's all these people in the water. They're floating around. And the helicopter, there's something up there. Who do I save? There's 100 people drowning. I don't know who to save. Who do I save? OK, that's the question. Who do you save? Who are you actually trying to help? Who are you trying to serve? And for a helicopter pilot, guess who it is they try to save? They look down, and they find the people that are swimming towards them. And that's who they save. So for you, it's the same thing. You can save everybody. Your stuff is amazing, right? The only thing you can really save are people swimming towards you. So who are the people that are most likely to be coming to me? That's who I got to figure out who those people are. That's who I'm going to serve. And what's cool about it, as you start doing this, you start serving people. You're going through this process documentation. They're going to see, hey, the helicopter's pulling out people that are swimming towards the helicopter. I should probably swim that way. And more people will come. More people will come. The impact you have will increase. But it starts with coming out the people that are swimming to you, who's most predisposed to buy your thing. You can serve them, find those people first. And that's how the process works. And then it's up to you guys to become that person that they're going to follow. All right. So now we've got number one kind of there. And again, I talk about this concept in both Dockham Secrets and Expert Secrets book. It's the only concept I think we talk about in both books. It's vitally important to this being successful. So now number two, we're shifting over to is this right here, the future-based cause or the culture. To start this one off, I want to show you this video that started this whole joke about starting your own cult. So this is how many of you guys here remember the CrossFit cult? OK, so this video started the whole thing here inside of our office. Here we go. So I says to her, I says. OK, before you go farther, why are you talking like this? Excuse me, CrossFitter's coming through. Oh, God. Ew. You're so sweaty. Yeah. We just got back from the box. Hardwad, hardbod. I just hit 6,000 burpees, and I feel great. Dude, that is not OK. Sorry, I'm making a smoothie. That's fine. It's paleo. I don't care. Greenish, kiwi, salmon, ham, protein, leprosy, leprosy, leprosy. I don't care. Well, I don't care that you don't care. First rule of CrossFit. Always talk about CrossFit. CrossFit's not just an exercise. It's a cult. I mean way of life. Did you say it's a cult? Yo, M, spot me. One for the dance group. Two photos to Instagram. Can you give me three? Three photos to Instagram. Yeah, you guys just exercise without showing off? Nope. Facebook tweeted reddit. Oh, Jesus. They're everywhere. Murph, kettlebell swings. Go. God. Love the pain. The pain is your only friend. Angel yourself. You guys seem really sick and hurt. I'm fine. I'm fine. It's fine. Stop, guys. Guys, stop. This is disturbing. I respect that you have this new hobby you're really excited about. You don't have to let it change your personalities. Wrong. CrossFit is not a hobby. It's a cult. You said it's a cult. Yeah, you said cult again. No. It's a hobby. It's the second time you've said it's a cult. 50-pin will do, Burby's trapped. The floor is covered in vomit. I'm not okay. No. What are you doing? I don't know. There's just so much social pressure in your CrossFit. Yeah, but that doesn't mean you have to do it. It's actually a pretty good workout. You should try it sometime. Not you, too. CrossFit. All right. So someone showed up that video, and I was dying laughing, because I had five friends all in CrossFit at the time. And then this is about the time that we were talking about this internally as a company. That was a joke. It's like, how do we create our own culture? And it's based off of that. And then you've got the cult shirt on. How many of you said the cult shirts on? Just one right now. So in our last fucking live event, we talked about them and gave everybody these t-shirts to say, it's a cult. I mean, way of life. And then hashtag, it's a cult on the back. So that's been kind of our internal joke over the last year and a half inside of our company. And it's like, how do we build a cult following the people? Like build a culture, people that are excited, that are passionate about what we're doing, right? And that started, that concept, that question, started a whole bunch of stuff. And again, we knew the pattern. And now these are things we've done to amplify it. And there's so many good stories in the Expert Seekers book of other people that have done this inside of our group now. Brandon and Katelyn are one of the best examples they've done with Lady Boss weight loss. They've built this huge, crazy cult following the people now who buy everything they say. They're having huge success, huge results. It's amazing. Ray Higdon just came, joined our industry a little while ago. And he understood this process. Like yeah, he's been selling training programs to this market forever, made really good money, but he didn't have a culture of people. So he's like, what can I do? What can I do? And they've created something about four or five months ago called the rank makers. And you can become a rank maker. And you rank up in your thing. And as he launched that, I'm just watching his company just completely explode. Because he's building an internal culture for their customers to be part of. And so that's kind of where we get at. So a couple things. And I've got a couple different lines here that you guys are going to be writing through before next walk and talk. So as you're paying attention, look down here. I'm going to have you guys write down what your future-based causes, your campaign slogan, your formant mile, and your self-identifier. So I'm going to talk through these with my company. And then we're going to have you guys write them. Then we're going to do the next walk and talk. So start looking at that. So as I was writing this chapter in the book, it was in the middle of the elections with Trump and Hillary. And it was fascinating because I'm writing all these things about how big mass movements have been built. And I'm writing this section on how do you, every mass movement, it's all about a future-based thing. Never are the causes like, here we're going to make this thing better. The word ER, we'll talk about these opportunities, better, faster. It's not that. What gets people to move is we're going this direction. If you read The True Believers, which is one of the best books on building mass movements and stuff like that, you talk about in traditional, in schooling system, you go from grade, the first grade to the second grade to the third grade. And companies, you start at the bottom, you work right to the next level. And people, they kind of ascend through things slowly. But if you want to create a movement, it's not about getting people to slowly move things. It's getting them to take this huge step. They have to have something to put their hope and their faith into that's going to get them to actually move. And the more I was studying, I noticed that none of the movements were ever talking about present-based stuff. They just don't. They always talk about this is where we're going. Every single one of them. You look for them, again, religion, spiritual, cults, businesses, they're always talking about where they're going. And so I'm right in this section. I'm taking a different case study. And I look over, and we had the TV was on, but the sound was muted. And I look over, and it's the Trump-Hillary debates. And I'm looking at this, I'm like, huh. And I don't know who's going to win at the time, but I'm looking at their campaign slogans. And if you look at this, Hillary's, her slogans were fighting for us, present-based. I'm with her. It was another one, which I don't even know what that is. That was the worst one ever. Trump was, let's make America great again. Future-based college. We're going to do this thing. We're going to make this America great again, right? Future-based. Hillary's very, very present-based. I was like, huh. And I thought Trump was going to win, because he's a better marketer, but I had no idea who was going to win. And now it's kind of fun looking backwards, like, OK, future-based caused one. But at the time, I was watching this, and I was like, I wonder, historically, if I looked at all the different presidential elections from George Washington down to now, like, what were people's campaign slogans? And so I went to Wikipedia, and I started looking through, and I found all these things. If you look at this, George Bush, who was going as Bill Clinton, George Bush was a proud tradition, present-based, right? Bill Clinton is time to change America. Future-based. Future-based. John McCain, country-first. Barack Obama, change, right? It didn't matter Democrat or Republican, almost 100% accuracy, all the elections back from the beginning of time until now. The person whose message was based on a future-based cause won, and the person who had a present-based cause lost. Nobody wants to, if you're picking a new government official, it's all about the change, right? That's what you're trying to do. Everyone wants that kind of thing. So I started looking at that, like, presidential elections, I started looking at other things, right? And this is about the time that we were doing our last Funnel Hacking Live event. And I was like, what is our future-based cause? What gets people inspired in our community? I didn't have something at the time. And I remember I was writing a headline. There was an old Gary Halbert headline that said, we won sales letter away from being rich. And I remember I started writing a headline on the page. It was like, you're one funnel away from being rich. And I was like, I think it's so shallow. And I'm like, ugh. People that I've responded to, that's probably not who I want to hang out with, right? So I was like, OK, well, you won funnel away from having financial freedom. I'm like, OK, that's a little better. But that's not everyone's reason why they do this. We won funnel away from having the impact you need on the world. Like, that's some people's. I kept going through these different things. And I was like, what is it? And then I remember I deleted the end of the sentence, or I think the next thing. On top of it, it said, you're just one funnel away. And I was like, oh my gosh, that's it for us. That's my slogan. For everyone coming in here, you won funnel away from whatever it is for you. Some of you guys just quit firing your boss. Some of it's changing the world. Some of it's getting your product, your service out. Everyone's got everything, right? So that became our thing. In fact, Stephen, do you have your phone on you? Yeah. We pulled the marketers group. I'm going to show Jamie's story. So Jamie posted this in the inner circle two days ago. So Jamie crossed. He joined inner circle a little while ago. And she posted this two days ago. So it said, six months ago, I hopped on a plane to Funnel Hacking Live with my breast pump and a lot of paper for taking good notes. I don't know why she didn't tell us that part of it, but whatever. OK, my husband was about to leave his comfortable job teaching and coaching, wrestling. And we were going to jump off a cliff and go full-time entrepreneurship, even though if any smart business guy looked at our numbers, we would have said it was a dumb decision. Our business was six years old. It was time to take the ultimate risk. Funnel Hacking Live changed everything. I saw Russell, Kaelin, Garrett, a bunch of other heroes who made me believe that I was really just one funnel away. Six months later, a lot of sleepless nights, few resilient live-perfect webinars, expert secrets and dot com secrets books, few other times, and a really ugly funnel. I'm happy to say that I built myself. We've gone from $0,000 to $90,000 in six weeks. The amazing thing is that Russell's marketing principles also spilled over to my website, which means combined with my funnel, we've done over $130,000 in the last six weeks. The longest overnight success ever. I sat there in those final hours when Tony Robbins said, turn to your buddy and imagine the moment you've dreamt about for years and act like it just happened. Despite the feeling totally awkward, I closed my eyes and saw a small feat of my boys hit the soil on the ranch. Nathan and I had been dreaming about for 15 years and imagined it was ours. I turned to my new friend Rachel and screamed and cried so hard that my outside voice forgot about my inside voice and let go. Something happened. Right now, we're on track to $1 million a month by the end of January. And the plans for impact are in full motion. When I saw the ads for Funnel Hacking Live, I'd watch them over and over again holding on to those words. You're just one funnel away. An ads guy I hired back then said, you don't really bind all that Russell funnel garbage, do you? Well, he doesn't work for me anymore. Now to be here with all of you six months later, just amazing people who are killing it. Anyway, that's the impact that the right thing can have on your audience. It causes them to believe. Half of our job in this business is to get people to believe that the result we're trying to promise them is actually possible. There's a reason why I'm always doing all these crazy things in Facebook Live to show my whole goal. My job in this business is to get people to believe this is real. And if you don't think that I believe it's real, I'm not doing my job right. I am convinced that this is the only thing that matters for entrepreneurs. What we're doing, this is the most important thing. It's why I go crazy. It's why I don't sleep at nights. It's because I believe it. I want you guys to believe as well. So this came out. And that's just one story of somebody. But it's given people a place to put their hope and their faith. If they're going to join your movement, they want the result. They want the change. They want this future-based thing in the future. What is that thing that you have? So I always try to think about this. If I was to be running for president of my market, what would be my campaign slogan? So for me, it's, you're going funnel away. That's my campaign slogan. And this year, we're trying to figure out, should we do a new one for Fun Locking Live? I was like, no. This message is for us. This is the message. This is where we're taking people. So that's number one, is figuring that out. So I started thinking through that. In a minute, you have time to write that out. But if you were to run for president of your market and try to get people to leave whatever it is that they're currently doing and come and follow you, what's your campaign slogan? Why should they listen to you? OK. Step number two, you've got to give people a goal. You've got to give people the ability to break their four-minute mile. And obviously, I'm sure most of you guys have heard the story of the dude who broke four-minute mile, right? Roger Bannister was the first person to do it. Prior to that, nobody thought it was physically possible for a human to break four-minute mile. They tried for how many 6,000-plus years since the beginning of time until now. And finally, this dude breaks it. And after he breaks it, everyone's like, huh, this is possible. And guess what happened? Like, in the next year, like, 15 other people broke it right afterwards, because it was possible. And look back for me. Like, when I got in this business, my goal initially was to make $1,200 a month because that would replace my wife's salary. She was crazy. That's all she was making back then. I feel bad. Anyway, I was like the husband and wrestler who wasn't helping at all. So I was like, if I can make $1,200 a month, then she can quit her job. That was my goal. That was it. That's all I aspire towards. $1,200 a month, and my wife can quit her job. And then one day, a little over 10 years ago, 10 years ago this year, I have a guy who's now become a friend. His name's John Reese. I mean, I've heard this story before. But John set out a goal to make $1,000,000 in a day, which I didn't know that was his goal. I didn't know anything about it. And I was on vacation down in southern Idaho. And the place we were staying at was before cell phones and internet and all that kind of stuff. So I had maybe two customers at the time. I remember the whole week freaking out. Like, what if my customers emailed me? I need to get somewhere to check my email. So we finally found a little tiny library down there this place. We went to the library. And I logged in. You walk up, and there's like four or five seats, and there's like one computer. It's a dial-up modem. So there's a line of people all waiting for their turn. It's like 10-minute maximum. I'm like, awesome. Waiting in line. Waiting in line. Finally, I get down. I sit down on the computer. I check my email. It's downloading, downloading, and looking at it. And none of my customers emailed me. But I got one email that came in that said we did it. That was the subject line from John Reese. And I was like, we did what? Like, I don't know what we did. So look at it. And there's this picture of him. Somebody I saw in the last webinar he sent me the picture. This picture of him sitting there in front of a little white board, and it said $1 million goal. And they had crossed. And he said, we launched this course. And in the first 18 hours, we passed $1 million in sales. And I remember sitting there in that chair in this little tiny library in Bear Lake, Idaho, sitting there. And we're sitting back. And I was like, this guy, John Reese, made $1 million in a day. I was like, my goal is $1,200 a month. Oh, man. I'm like, if I do the math, I'm like, he's had a $1,000 course. He's sold 1,000 copies of it. Like, maybe I can't do 1,000 copies in a day. But what if I did it in a year? I could make $1 million a year. What if it took me five years? That's like $200,000 a year. And all of a sudden, this thing became possible in my head. And I started thinking differently, started looking different. And all of a sudden, I was like, oh my gosh, I can do that. And now my goal has shifted. Because John Reese did it. I knew that I could do it. And so because he did it, I went out there. And within a year of graduating from college, I made over $1 million. And eventually, I got to $1 million in the year. Later, I did $1 million in a month. Another time, I did $1 million a week. And there's multiple times I've done $1 million in a day. And once, I did $1 million an hour. And I'm going to try to do $10 million at Cardone's event in February, which would be even cooler. But because it was real for him, that became real for me. I was like, I can do it. It wasn't this impossible thing. And so as we were building click funnels, again, a lot of these things didn't come out when we launched the company. You guys are having a good spot because you can build these foundational things while you're doing stuff. We were just kind of doing them. And it was one day, we were talking about this. And I asked Dave, how many people in ClickFunnels have hit a million bucks? I have no idea. So this dude named Tracy, who glows in the database and pulls out on numbers. So Tracy's in the database. He pops it out. And he was like, 50-something people. I was like, what? With 50 people that made a million bucks in ClickFunnels? He's like, yeah. I didn't know that. We need to talk. It's a big deal. We should talk about that. So other people know that it's not just Russell making money in ClickFunnels. It's a whole bunch of people. And so we made these really cool awards you guys probably saw them in the hallway in the bathroom, where when they break a million dollars in a funnel, we send this huge trophy to the Two Comic Club Award. And they get one on the wall. And we get a mini version that we put in the hallway here. And the Fun Hiking Live. By the time Fun Hiking Live started last year, I think we had 93 people. And how many of you guys were in the room during that time? So we had the entire room full of people. And I thought when we did the award ceremony, everyone was going to leave. There was going to be people in the hallway talking. Nobody moved. The entire time we had a line of 93 people come up on stage. Each person gave them Two Comic Club Award. And they went off person after person after person. Nobody budged from the stage. And afterwards, guess what I saw in the forums? And the mess of everyone. It's like, I'm going to get Two Comic Club Award. I'm going to get Two Comic Club Award. That became the thing. Everyone saw that it wasn't just Russell making money. That all their peers sitting around them, like, you made a million bucks. And you did. Everyone's doing it. I should be able to do this too, right? And suddenly we broke the form in a mile for everyone. And everyone started going after it. And if you look in the hallway there, we're almost 200 people now. We're getting about one person a day breaking the million dollar mark every single day. It's like clockwork. It gets crazy because people believed. So it's exciting, right? You can give around to farms. Cool. [?]. My question for you guys, within what you're selling, what is it? What's the goal people can shoot towards? What form in a mile can you give people to go for? If you don't have something that they're striving towards within your organization, within your culture, they're going to leave you. They're going to come by the thing, get the thing, and they're going to leave. Because you create something inside of what you're doing that inspires them to do more. They're going to keep doing it inside of you. We have people right now who have huge teams, like Agora Financial. So crazy. The Agora Financial division did $200 million this year. Agora as a whole, there's over $1 billion a year, right? So I met lunch last week with six of the main marketing guys from Agora Financial. And they told me that, yeah, we have a ClickFunnels account. Every month we submit a receipt to our financial to tell them why we have it. And I was like, you guys have a whole team of designers and programmers. Why don't you just think you don't need our software? And the girl said, no, I want to win a two comic book award. So I'm making them shift all of our products to your thing now. $200 million a year there, and they want a two comic book award. Like it's crazy, right? But it's not. That's what people want. They aspire to those things, right? And so right now, like I said, we should have the next Funnel Like a Lab probably 200 plus people who are going to come on stage and get an award. And for everybody else in the audience, they're going to see that, and they're going to believe. And the next year it's going to be bigger, and it's going to be bigger. In fact, this year our biggest problem is all these people, like we have people who have gotten five or six two comic book awards, and they're like, this is too easy. So we're launching an eight-figure award. We've got five people right now on ClickFunnels made over eight figures in one of their funnels. So we're doing that. Now it's the next bar. Now they're like, oh my gosh, those five people who just like me did $10 million in a funnel. And eventually we'll keep going and keep going, right? But give them the ability to break the four-in-a-mile. So for you, what is it you have inside your organization, inside of what you're doing that people can strive towards to do it? Is it a weight loss goal? Is it a financial goal? It's like, what is it you have that can get people to strive towards something? OK, so think through that. The next piece here is I want to get people to self-identify with the cause. Think about the things that you're part of, like you believe in. I'm a Mormon. So I identify with that cause. Like that's who I am. I'm a wrestler. I identify with that. It's who I am. I'm a biohacker. I believe in that. I identify with that. What are the things you personally identify with? You all have them, right? So inside your organization, you create some people self-identified so they can be part of what you are. Again, this happened initially, but we're trying to figure out a way to decrease our churns. We started giving people a funnel hacker t-shirt when they joined. And it was expensive. We're spending $10 for every t-shirt. I cover the shipping and the printing. All they have to do is create a click, thumbs account, watch a 10 minute video, and I ship them a t-shirt. So I go in the hole on these things. And after about four or five weeks of doing this, my accounting guy came in and was like, do we're spending so much money on t-shirts? Like, can we not do that? And I was like, probably a good idea. I don't know. I don't know if the impact is making or whatever. About that time, I got messaged with some guy. It was so funny on Facebook. He's like, hey, Russell. And he had pictures. Like, hey, I'm a funnel hacker. And he called himself a funnel hacker. I'm like, oh, cool. And I was like, that's the coolest. I'm glad you got the shirt. I love it. He's like, you know what's funny about this? He's like, I signed up for ClickFunnels three months ago. I got the shirt. I wear the shirt almost every day. My wife drives her crazy. But I love this shirt. I love the movement. I feel like I'm a funnel hacker. I've never actually logged into ClickFunnels since that first time. But I keep paying you every single month because I'm a funnel hacker. And I want to be part of that movement. I don't want to lose my membership card, right? I was like, how fascinating is that? How do people identify with your cause? Like, I'm a funnel hacker. I build funnels. All the stuff we do, if you look at our t-shirts, our stuff, I'm trying to people self-identify with our movement. First time I talked about this inner circle meeting when Kalen and Brandon were sitting in the room. At the time, their business was called Tool Time Trainer. They're doing super good. I can't remember. $200, $300,000 a year, a good business. And they're sitting there. And she's sitting there. She's like, nobody's going to identify with me. My company's Tool Time Trainer. Her last name was Tool. No one's saying, I'm a Tool Time Trainer. They can't self-identify because this is me. She's like, how do I create something different they can self-identify with? So she's sitting in the plane. They're flying the plane. She's like, how do I create something that self-identify with? What is it? What is it? What is it? And on the plane she had to differ, what she called Lady Boss Weight Loss. And she's like, I want my people to say that I'm Lady Boss. I'm a Lady Boss. That's who I am. This is a new identity shift in their life. You want people, when they come into your culture, to have an identity shift. If you look at the negative side of mass movements, you look at colts, when they bring people in, what do they do? They give people identity shifts. They have them leave. They do all sorts of crazy things to give them to have an identity shift. So you've got the negative side. How do you do it positively? So how do you get them to self-identify with you and your movement? And so the easiest way to do this is make sure something they can put on a t-shirt. That's how people are like, I'm going to have them self-identify as the da, da, da, da, da. Can they put that on a t-shirt? They can't put on a t-shirt. It's no good. It's got to be so simple that I can have a t-shirt. I'm a funnel hacker. I build funnels. So think about that. If you can put your thing, the self-identified thing, on a t-shirt that they'll wear. If they'll wear it. And look right now. Myron's got to build funnels on. We've got how many guys are wearing? We've got a funnel hacker. How many guys only wear a ClickFunnels clothing lines now? My wife's like, I drink the tears of my enemies. How many guys identify with that one? Anyway, so think about that. What inside of your organization is people's stuff identified with you? One of the reasons why this is important, when we launch ClickFunnels and start having success, guess what started happening really, really quickly? Everyone in my market's like, dude, Russell's making a ton of money with this. I'm going to build the next ClickFunnels killer. Since we launched ClickFunnels, I think, I don't know the exact number. It's like 40 plus people have launched ClickFunnels killers. I got literally the subject line. I get everyone of them. Everyone forwards them to me. So the next ClickFunnels killer, and they launched a new product. How many of these guys are around right now? None of them. You want to know why? Because my people are funnel hackers. Funnel hackers don't use other crappy software. They see come out, and they laugh at it, and they come back to ClickFunnels, because they identify with us. That's how we build this net around us, so people don't go to other people for their software. We've created something where they identify with us. Noah, one of our interstitial members, he told me, he said, every single time I see a Facebook ad for the next ClickFunnels killer, he's like, I pause long enough just to laugh, and then to scroll past it. He's like, I'm a funnel hacker. If you self-identify, it doesn't matter what. This protects you from competition. There's people coming after me every single day. So it's like, the more you people self-identify with us and our cause and our movement, the less likely they are to leave. Because if they leave, they've got to leave this whole identity behind them. That's hard. So we build an identity, help them self-identify with you. Then one more thing. This one's on your paperwork. This one takes a little longer. I want to share this as well, is having some type of, I call it the title, liberty. And I'll tell you why here in a second. But something that people can look towards is like, a standard for them to move towards. And if you look inside of the Extra Secrets book, I show Kaelin's example. I show ours, a bunch of other ones. I call it the title, liberty. And the reason why, for the Mormons and Ramin, know the story. Those of you who aren't, I'm just going to share it really quick. So in the Book of Mormon, which is a book about the ancient Americans here in America, and they're dealing with God and their wars and everything like that. And in the book, there's this war that's happening. There's a guy named Captain Moroni. And Captain Moroni, he is in a war that he's losing. Getting beat up, his entire army is getting demolished by everybody else. It got so bad that the people on his army, who were going out to go fight with him, were like, we're getting killed. So they'd sneak off at night to go to the other guys, and they're like, I'm going to fight with you guys. And they come back and fight their own team, because they were getting beat so bad. And he's like, the captain of this army, he's like, I've got to help my people to win. What am I supposed to do? I'm getting destroyed here, right? I'm going into a war that I shouldn't win. There's no possible way. How do I get my people? How do I get my troops inspired enough they're going to follow me into this war? So what did he do? He took the coat off his back. He ripped his coat. And honest to code, he wrote this down. He said, in memory of our God, our religion, our freedom, and our peace, our wives and our children, he fastened to a pole and he held it out there. He said, guys, this is why we're fighting. Come on back. People came, and they were inspired, and they had what they needed, and they went into this war, and they won. And so for me, I'm always like, business is a war. We're always going through these wars every single day. So it's like, how do you get your people? And this works from a company level, from employees, but even more so, like, your culture, your people. Like, how do you get them to come stick with you to follow you, right? And so you're creating these tidal liberties. So again, inside the Expert Signers book, we have different examples. There's a lot of ways to do this. I'm going to share one, because this is probably my favorite one we built. It's the video. And this video is like a trailer on a whole bunch of videos we do, and some of you guys have probably seen it before. But when you hear this, let me know if this calls out to you. Like, is it me? You're like, that's me. That's my people. I want to go to war with those guys. Like, that's me. How do you self-identify it? And when you hear this, think that, like, does this draw you in? OK? I want you to just create stuff like this, because this is like your tidal liberties. I'm just going to get people to come to you. OK? Here's the video. My name is Russell Brunson. And I'm part of an underground group of entrepreneurs that you've probably never even heard of before. We don't rely on cash from venture capitalists to get started. And we don't even have goals to go public either. In fact, our motivation is the exact opposite. You see, we have products and services and things that we know change people's lives. Because we're fighting against the big brands, people with literally unlimited budgets, we have to do things differently. We have to do things smarter. We don't have financial safety nets, because every test we take is with our own money. We have to be profitable from day number one. So how do we do that? How is that even possible? If you ask the MBAs or look at your college textbooks, they will tell you what we are doing is impossible to get us happening every single day. And it's happening through an art and a science that we call funnel hacking. It's not really something that I can explain to you. It's something I have to show you. These stories are a glimpse of entrepreneurs just like you, who have a dream and a vision, who want to change the world in their own middle way. I use the funnel hacking process to help make their dreams become a reality. Yes, we are funnel hackers, and these are our stories. I love that video. All right. OK, so this is the slide right now, you guys. I want you to spend five minutes or so writing down for your own cause, this movement you're trying to create. OK, what is the cause? Like, what are you trying to get people to do? OK? My cause is this. I want to help entrepreneurs. I want to help business. I want to help people lose weight. This is my cause. And within that, if you're running for president of that market, what's your slogan? What is your platform? Are you going to be pitching these people on? Why should they be following you versus your competitors? After that, what kind of format mile could you create? It gets people to aspire towards something they want to try to achieve. And last one is how they self-identify. What could you actually put on a t-shirt? I am a funnel hacker. I'm a lady boss. I'm a rank maker. I'm a think of the markets. Anyone else have theirs done? That's cool. They have a t-shirt for their business. I'm a biohacker. Anyway, so those are the four things. You guys got that?
